1.exposition: The long debate over the strong and the weak comes to rest not in a rule but in a person. Paul, ranging himself among the strong, lays down their charge: to bear the failings of the weak and not to please themselves, and instead to please the neighbor for his good and upbuilding (v.2). The warrant is the deepest possible: Christ did not please himself, but bore the reproaches aimed at God. A word on the Scriptures follows — they were written to give endurance, encouragement, and hope (v.4) — and then a prayer that the God of endurance would grant the strong and weak such harmony that they might, with one voice, glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.
1.historical_context: 'We who are strong' (v.1) is Paul's self-identification with the freer party — largely Gentile believers who no longer reckon the Torah's food laws and holy days binding — over against the 'weak,' mainly Jewish Christians still scrupulous about them (the dispute of ch. 14). The Psalm 69:9 quoted in v.3 is a psalm of the righteous sufferer, read of the Messiah's passion. The divine titles that frame the section — 'the God of endurance and encouragement' (v.5) and 'the God of hope' (v.13) — are unusual epithets that name God as the very source of the graces a divided community most needs.
1.christ: 'Christ did not please himself' (v.3) is the most compressed christological warrant in Paul's ethics. The whole descent — incarnation, ministry, cross — was an act of not-self-pleasing, the same self-emptying he sets before the Philippians (Philippians 2:5–8). The righteous sufferer of Psalm 69 bore reproaches not his own, interposing himself where the insult fell. So the strong are to interpose themselves for the weak, taking the inconvenience upon themselves rather than pressing it on those less able to bear it.

verses.note: Paul ranges himself with the mature: we who are strong ought to bear the failings of the weak, and not to please ourselves. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson press the verb — to bear is to carry, support, endure patiently, not merely to tolerate — and the strong are the robust in faith, free in conscience over meats and days. reformed Calvin turns it to the law of love: strength is lent for service, not self-indulgence, a debt the strong owe the weak.

Let each of us please his neighbor for his good, to build him up. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson guard the rule from abuse: the pleasing is not flattery or indulgence of sin but is fenced by two limits — the neighbor's good and his edification. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret hold the balance: we accommodate ourselves to others, yet always toward their upbuilding, never mere man-pleasing.

The ground of it all is the Master: Christ did not please himself. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that Paul roots the whole ethic in Christ's example — the highest sought not his own ease but the Father's will and our salvation — and proves it from Psalm 69:9: 'the reproaches of those who reproached you fell on me.' reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret draw the force: if the Lord of all bore reproach for others, how much more must his servants forgo their pleasure for the brethren.

The citation prompts a great aside: whatever was written in former days was written for our instruction. grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Clarke read this as a charter for the abiding use of the Old Testament — designed by God for every later generation, not only its first hearers — that through the endurance and encouragement the Scriptures supply we might have hope. reformed Calvin adds a sharp polemic: this condemns any who would discard the Old Testament, for Paul testifies God appointed it for our salvation.

May the God of endurance and encouragement grant you to live in harmony, in accord with Christ Jesus. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the title is drawn straight from v.4 — God is the giver of the very patience and consolation the Scriptures convey. reformed Calvin and puritan Henry weigh 'according to Christ Jesus': the likemindedness sought is patterned on Christ, not a mere worldly truce.

The goal of the harmony is worship: that together you may with one voice glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark 'one mind and one mouth' as the fruit of unity — unanimous, harmonious praise. eastern Cyril presses the point: the end of all forbearance among the brethren is the common glorifying of God; division mars his praise, concord magnifies it.

witnesses.point: Paul, numbering himself with the strong, lays down their duty — to bear and support the infirmities of the weak, denying themselves for the weaker brother's good.
Strength is given for service, not self-pleasing; the likemindedness sought is patterned 'according to Christ,' not a mere worldly agreement.
We are to accommodate ourselves to others, yet always toward their edification, never mere man-pleasing.
The likemindedness is to be 'according to Christ Jesus' — conformity to him is the pattern and measure of Christian unity.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: The 'strong' — Paul among them, largely Gentile and free of scruple — are obliged to carry the 'weak,' mainly Jewish believers; Christ's refusal to please himself, proved from Psalm 69, is the theological engine, and the appeal drives toward the community's unified worship.

1.application: The church is not a society of the equally mature competing in liberty; it is a body in which the strong carry the weak, in imitation of the One who carried the world's weight. Real strength is measured by what it will bear, and for whom.

7.pericope_label: Christ the Hope of Jews and Gentiles
7.exposition: Paul lifts the local quarrel over food into the widest possible frame: welcome one another as Christ has welcomed you. For Christ came as a servant to the circumcised, confirming the promises to the fathers, and so that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. A fourfold chain of Scripture — from the Law, the Psalms, and the Prophets — proves that Gentile praise alongside Israel was always God's design, climaxing in the root of Jesse who rises to rule the nations and in whom they hope. The paraenesis then closes with a benediction to the God of hope, who fills the believing with joy and peace and makes their hope abound by the Spirit's power.
7.original_language: The four citations are a deliberate catena, gathered as the synagogue and Qumran gathered testimonia, and drawn from the Greek Scriptures across all three divisions of the canon: the Psalms (Psalm 18:49; Psalm 117:1), the Law (Deuteronomy 32:43), and the Prophets (Isaiah 11:10). Each names the ethnē (the nations, Gentiles) praising God. The climactic text follows the Septuagint: where the Hebrew of Isaiah 11:10 has the nations 'seek' the root of Jesse, the Greek reads that they will hope in him (elpiousin) — the very word Paul seizes for the title 'the God of hope' (ho theos tēs elpidos) in v.13.
7.historical_context: The chain (Psalm 18:49; Deuteronomy 32:43; Psalm 117:1; Isaiah 11:10) shows that Jew-Gentile worship together is the fulfillment of Israel's own Scriptures, not a departure from them — the eschatological reality the prophets awaited. The 'root of Jesse' is a recognized messianic title for the Davidic deliverer. Coming after chs. 9–11, the section is the theological climax of the letter's long argument that God has been faithful to Israel and merciful to the nations in one and the same Christ.
7.christ: 'Christ became a servant to the circumcised' (v.8): the incarnation itself is service, not privilege — the Son took Jewish flesh and lived under the law to confirm the promises to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and through that covenant-faithfulness opened the door of mercy to the Gentiles. The root of Jesse in whom the nations hope is the risen Christ now exercising the Davidic rule Isaiah foresaw; and the joy, peace, and abounding hope of v.13 are poured out by the Spirit of that same risen Messiah.

verses.note: Paul gathers the whole exhortation into a single command: welcome one another as Christ has welcomed you, for the glory of God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the sweep — strong and weak alike are to embrace one another across every difference. reformed Calvin fixes the measure: as Christ received us, freely and while we were unworthy, Jew and Gentile alike. eastern Theodoret adds the end — this mutual welcome, like Christ's, redounds to God's glory.

Christ became a servant to the circumcised to show God's truthfulness, to confirm the promises given to the fathers. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that the Messiah's earthly ministry was to the Jews — born under the law, sent to Israel. eastern Chrysostom hears in it the vindication of God's faithfulness: his coming proved God true to his covenant word. reformed Calvin draws the doctrine — since Christ fulfilled the promises to the patriarchs, the old covenant was spiritual, not merely earthly.

The second design: that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. grammatical-historical Barnes and eastern Chrysostom mark the contrast with v.8 — to the Jews for God's truth (the promises), to the Gentiles for God's mercy (who had no promises). Robertson notes Paul now opens a chain of citations, the first from Psalm 18:49: 'I will praise you among the Gentiles.' eastern Theodoret finds the humbling point — the Gentiles owe their salvation to mercy alone.

The second witness: Rejoice, O Gentiles, with his people (Deuteronomy 32:43). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the Gentiles are called to rejoice not apart from Israel but with her — the two joined in one people, sharing one joy. reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley read the prophecy as foretelling the union of Jew and Gentile, a scriptural ground for their mutual reception.

A third: Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles (Psalm 117:1). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the doubled summons — 'all you Gentiles... all you peoples' — underscoring the breadth: every nation called to praise. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin observe that the sheer accumulation of witnesses puts the calling of the Gentiles beyond dispute.

The chain is crowned by the prophet: the root of Jesse will come, he who rises to rule the Gentiles (Isaiah 11:10). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify the 'root of Jesse' as the Messiah sprung from David's line, who rises — in resurrection and exaltation — to reign over the nations. eastern Cyril and reformed Calvin fasten on the last clause: 'in him will the Gentiles hope' — Christ the object of the nations' faith and the ground of their salvation.

The prophecies of Gentile hope break into prayer: may the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the title is drawn from the hope just foretold in v.12. puritan Henry marks 'in believing' — faith the channel through which joy and peace flow; reformed Calvin the end and agent — the abounding hope is no human achievement but the work of the Holy Spirit's power.

witnesses.point: Christ's coming to the Jews vindicated God's truthfulness by fulfilling the promises to the fathers; the Gentiles, lacking the promises, are saved by mercy alone.
Paul strings a chain of Old Testament citations to prove that Gentile praise alongside Israel was foretold across the Scriptures.
As Christ received us freely while we were unworthy, Jew and Gentile alike, so we are to receive one another, to the glory of God.
The 'root of Jesse' rises to reign over the nations, and in him the Gentiles hope — Christ the object of their faith and the ground of their salvation.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: The four citations, drawn from Law, Psalms, and Prophets, gather Jew and Gentile into a single worshiping family — the theological climax of the whole letter, of which 'welcome one another' is the practical upshot.

7.application: A community that welcomes across every line of difference is not merely practicing an inclusive policy; it is displaying the God who welcomes. Those whom Christ has received at the cost of his life cannot finally refuse one another.

14.pericope_label: Paul the Minister to the Gentiles
14.exposition: The letter turns personal. Paul assures the Romans of his high regard — full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, able to instruct one another — before owning that he has written boldly by way of reminder, warranted by the grace given him. That grace he defines in startling priestly terms: he is a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles, whose offering is the Gentiles themselves, sanctified by the Spirit. In Christ he has cause to boast (v.17), yet only of what Christ has accomplished through him — a Spirit-empowered ministry of signs and wonders reaching from Jerusalem around to Illyricum — and only along his pioneer principle: to preach where Christ has not been named, as Isaiah foretold.
14.original_language: Verse 16 is dense with the vocabulary of the temple. Paul calls himself a leitourgos — a public servant, the word used of priests in their liturgy — 'ministering as a priest' (hierourgounta) the gospel of God, so that the prosphora ('offering') of the Gentiles might be acceptable, hēgiasmenē ('sanctified, consecrated') by the Holy Spirit. It is a bold transfer of sacrificial language to the apostolic mission: the priest is Paul, the altar the gospel, the sacrifice the converted nations. 'I have fulfilled' the gospel (peplērōkenai, v.19) likewise means not that he reached every soul but that he brought the gospel to full course through the region.
14.historical_context: Paul's self-portrait as a priest whose offering is the Gentile churches is unique in his letters — a new-covenant counterpart to the temple service in Jerusalem. The geographic arc 'from Jerusalem and around to Illyricum' (v.19, the region along the Adriatic) marks the eastern sweep of his completed mission; Illyricum is not mentioned in Acts. The pioneer policy of v.20 explains why he has not yet come to an already-founded Roman church, and points beyond it to Spain, the western edge of the known world. The Isaiah 52:15 citation applies the Servant Song's reach to his own frontier preaching.
14.christ: 'What Christ has accomplished through me' (v.18) is Paul's constant framing: the apostle is the instrument, Christ the agent. The obedience of the Gentiles — by word and deed, signs and wonders, Spirit-power — is the risen Christ's own work done through a body given to his service. The priestly image guards it: as the Levite presented but did not create the offering, so Paul presents the Gentiles whom Christ himself has made a people by the Spirit. His mission is the fulfillment of the Servant's, of whom the nations 'had not heard' now seeing and understanding (Isaiah 52:15).

verses.note: Paul softens before he explains himself: I am satisfied that you yourselves are full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, able to instruct one another. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear a gracious courtesy — he does not think them ignorant or evil. eastern Chrysostom and reformed Calvin note the tact: crediting them with goodness and competence to admonish, he prepares them to receive his bold reminder graciously.

I have written to you rather boldly by way of reminder, because of the grace given me by God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the qualification — he writes not to teach the ignorant but to put them in mind of what they know. reformed Calvin grounds the boldness in Paul's apostleship: his warrant is the grace of his office, not presumption. eastern Theodoret admires the humility joined to authority.

The grace is defined in priestly terms: a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles in the priestly service of the gospel, that the offering of the Gentiles may be acceptable, sanctified by the Holy Spirit. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the words are drawn from temple service; grammatical-historical Ellicott, that the Gentiles themselves are the sacrifice Paul presents. reformed Calvin insists the priesthood is a figure — Paul offers men to God by winning them to the gospel, not, as the sacerdotalists claim, by offering up Christ.

In Christ Jesus, then, I have reason to be proud of my work for God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson allow a legitimate, holy boast in the fruit of his apostolic labor. reformed Calvin fixes the qualification — the glorying is 'through Jesus Christ,' who wrought all by him, not in Paul's own merit. puritan Henry and eastern Theodoret confine the boast to 'the things pertaining to God,' the sacred work, not worldly attainment.

He will claim no more than his due: I will not venture to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through me to bring the Gentiles to obedience. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the studied restraint — nothing ascribed to self, all to Christ's working. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret weigh 'by word and deed': the whole effective ministry, preaching and mighty works alike, was Christ's own act through his servant.

The means and reach: by the power of signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God, from Jerusalem and around to Illyricum. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the supernatural attestation and inward power that accompanied the preaching, and the vast circuit — a great sweep of the eastern empire. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin read 'fully preached' not as reaching every soul but as planting the gospel throughout the whole region.

His governing ambition: to preach the gospel where Christ has not been named, lest he build on another's foundation. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the pioneer's aim — to break new ground where the gospel had never come. reformed Calvin notes he would not labor where others had planted, and eastern Theodoret sees his special calling: to carry the gospel to fresh regions rather than reap where others had sown.

He grounds the policy in prophecy: those who have never been told of him will see, and those who have never heard will understand (Isaiah 52:15). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the Servant Song's reach applied to Paul's own frontier mission. eastern Cyril and reformed Calvin find in it the divine sanction: the calling of the unreached was foretold, and Paul's pioneer labor is its fulfillment.

witnesses.point: Paul describes his apostleship in priestly language drawn from temple service, presenting the converted Gentiles as an acceptable offering, and ascribes the whole work to Christ.
Paul's priesthood is a figure: he offers men to God by winning them to the gospel, not by any literal sacrifice — against the sacerdotal reading.
The Gentiles themselves are the offering the apostle-priest presents, consecrated by the Holy Spirit.
Paul writes boldly yet humbly, ascribing his right not to himself but to the grace of his apostolic office, and his special calling to break new ground among the Gentiles.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: The clustered cultic terms of v.16 — leitourgos, hierourgeō, prosphora — cast Paul's Gentile mission as a priestly liturgy, the converted nations laid on God's altar and sanctified by the Spirit.

14.application: Here is a theology of ministry: the gospel's work is not merely to announce but to present transformed lives as an offering to God. Its success is counted not in decisions tallied but in communities formed and consecrated — and the church needs both the pioneer who breaks new ground and the builder who consolidates it.

22.exposition: The pioneer principle is why Paul has so often been hindered from Rome; but now, his eastern work complete and his long desire ripe, he hopes to see them on his way to Spain. First, though, he must go to Jerusalem bringing aid to the saints — the contribution Macedonia and Achaia have gladly made for the poor there (v.26), a gift that is also a debt owed for the spiritual riches the Gentiles received through the Jews (v.27). Only when this is sealed will he come, in the fullness of the blessing of Christ. He begs their striving prayers for deliverance and acceptance in Judea, and closes with the benediction of the God of peace.
22.historical_context: The Jerusalem collection was Paul's most ambitious project — a material gift from his Gentile churches in Galatia, Macedonia, and Achaia to the mother church in Jerusalem, a concrete enactment of Jew-Gentile solidarity that he defends here and in 1 Corinthians 16:1–4 and 2 Corinthians 8–9. His anxiety over its reception (v.31) proved well-founded: Acts 21 records that his arrival in Jerusalem led to his arrest. Spain (v.24, 28) represents the western frontier of the known world; whether Paul ever reached it after his Roman imprisonment is historically uncertain.
22.christ: The 'fullness of the blessing of Christ' Paul hopes to bring (v.29) is finally the risen Christ himself — present through the Spirit, proclaimed by the apostle, received by faith. Spain, the western horizon, is the edge toward which the risen Lord's commission drives (Acts 1:8). The collection is the economic shape of a christological reality: the one body of Jew and Gentile, sharing what they have because they share in him. And the 'God of peace' who closes the section (v.33) is the God who, through his Son, made peace both with sinners and between the two peoples.

verses.note: The pioneer principle explains the long delay: this is why I have so often been hindered from coming to you. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note it was the press of his eastern labors, not neglect, that repeatedly kept him from Rome. eastern Theodoret adds that the hindrance sprang not from want of love but from the demands of his calling.

But now, since I no longer have any room for work in these regions, and since I have longed for many years to come to you. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that his pioneer work in the East being complete, he is at last free to turn westward. eastern Theodoret hears the affection — a longing for the Roman believers cherished over many years, now near its fulfillment.

I hope to see you in passing as I go to Spain, and to be helped on my journey there by you, once I have enjoyed your company for a while. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note Rome is a stopping-point on the way to the far west, and Paul hopes the church will speed him forward — a partnership in the mission. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin hear the warmth: he would first be refreshed by their fellowship.

First, an errand of mercy: at present I am going to Jerusalem, bringing aid to the saints. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that before Rome and Spain he must deliver the collection gathered for the poor believers. eastern Chrysostom and reformed Calvin mark the significance — this ministry of relief, binding Gentile and Jewish believers in practical love, was a priority he would not defer even for his longed-for visit.

Macedonia and Achaia have been pleased to make some contribution for the poor among the saints at Jerusalem. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the Greek churches freely chose to give; the Jerusalem believers, in poverty from persecution or famine, were relieved by their Gentile brethren. eastern Theodoret marks the gladness of it — 'they were pleased' — a free and cheerful gift.

Free, yet owed: they were pleased to do it, and indeed they owe it to them. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the paradox — a gift that is also a debt. reformed Calvin gives the ground: the gospel and every spiritual blessing came to the Gentiles from the Jews, so having shared in spiritual things they fittingly minister material ones. eastern Theodoret and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the equity — those who received the greater repay in the lesser.

When therefore I have completed this and have delivered to them what has been collected, I will leave for Spain by way of you. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the metaphor of 'sealing' the fruit — securely and certifiably conveying the gift into their hands. eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin mark the ordering: only after faithfully completing this ministry of mercy would he turn to Rome and Spain — duty before desire.

I know that when I come to you I will come in the fullness of the blessing of Christ. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the settled assurance about the character of his coming. reformed Calvin reads it of an abundant measure of the spiritual benefit Christ's gospel imparts, to be shared for their mutual edification.

I appeal to you, brothers, by our Lord Jesus Christ and by the love of the Spirit, to strive together with me in your prayers. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the strongest of motives urged, and press the verb — strive together is to wrestle, to agonize alongside him. reformed Calvin hears a call to fervent, striving intercession; puritan Henry marks the humility — the great apostle, facing danger, begs the prayers of the Roman saints.

Two perils named: that I may be delivered from the unbelievers in Judea, and that my service for Jerusalem may be acceptable to the saints. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the first was a real danger to his life, as the event proved (Acts 21). eastern Theodoret and reformed Calvin observe his clear foresight of both — the violent hostility of the unbelieving Jews and the possible suspicion of the believers toward his Gentile collection.

The hoped-for end: so that by God's will I may come to you with joy and be refreshed in your company. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the aim of a glad arrival, not one in sorrow or chains, and mark 'by the will of God' — Paul subordinates every plan to the divine pleasure. eastern Theodoret and wesleyan-arminian Wesley hear the longing for mutual refreshment in fellowship.

A closing benediction: may the God of peace be with you all. Amen. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the title is fitly chosen after a chapter so concerned with concord — God the author and giver of peace. eastern Theodoret marks the aptness: having urged mutual forbearance, Paul commends the strong and weak alike to the God whose presence alone can secure the harmony he has pressed throughout.

witnesses.point: Paul's eastern labors, not neglect, long hindered his visit; before Rome and Spain he must first carry the relief collection to the poor saints at Jerusalem.
The gospel and every spiritual blessing came to the Gentiles from the Jews, so having shared in spiritual things they rightly minister material ones; duty comes before Paul's own desire.
Paul foresaw both the hostility of the unbelievers and the possible suspicion of the believers, and sought prayer for both perils.
The great apostle, facing danger, humbly begs the striving prayers of the Roman believers — the strong needing the intercession of others.

external.name: Thomas R. Schreiner
external.point: The Jerusalem collection concretely embodies the Jew-Gentile unity the letter has argued for, and Paul's urgent plea for prayer betrays a real foreboding about his reception in Judea, with Spain marking the mission's western frontier.

22.application: Paul does not pretend to invincibility; he asks a church he has never met to labor with him in prayer, staking the outcome on God's will and their intercession. Mission is a shared work, and even an apostle needs to be carried by the prayers of the saints.

_meta.title: Welcome One Another as Christ Has Welcomed You
reflection: The strong 'ought to bear the failings of the weak, and not to please ourselves,' because 'Christ did not please himself' (15:1, v.3). Where are you spending your freedom on your own comfort rather than the building up of someone weaker?

'Welcome one another as Christ has welcomed you, for the glory of God' (v.7). Who is it costly for you to welcome — and how does measuring that welcome against Christ's welcome of you change the calculation?

Paul says the Gentiles, who came to 'share in' the Jews' spiritual blessings, 'owe it to them' to serve them materially (v.27). Where has grace reached you through another, and what concrete debt of love does that lay on you?
